of its readers, calling to them with an accent best described as “making it up as I go along.” The literature of simulation is a way of regulating the attentional forces arrayed around a work of literature, the

pleasures that draw it out into the world. Our encounter with a literary work is never one among equals, between reader and text. It is always one among many. Like any experience, it is a chain of signifiers

that we establish with our social environment. More precisely, it is a chain that we try to establish, and the text works to resist it at every turn. It is a chain of meaningful experience insofar as we make

and silences spread out between me and a work of literature: it is a set of interruptions, gestures, attentions, exchanges, expectations, and demands. The reader of a literary text is also a cultural media theor
forces that tend to make it what it isn’t, and make what it is, inconvenient. What if something else could happen, and something else could happen after that, and on and on, in an ever-larger

of the book form, creating content that can be endlessly recomposed, and is thus able to transcend its host’s physicality through the transformation of its surface, offering its reader the freedom of transformation. A verbal artifact has

virtually no other limitations and can retain its capacity for continuous metamorphosis as it shifts from one form to another. In literature, the author’s intent is realized in a highly ambiguous, unpredictable manner and may only be achieved

through the reader’s own interpretive effort. The reader is not a passive recipient of written text but its co-creator and curator. The ability to cultivate the book’s dual density, the materiality of the pages and

the conceptual nature of their content, is something that should be explored to the fullest extent possible.
The addition of interactive elements and interactivity is not the only way to achieve this goal. It is possible to advance the quality

of book design by introducing other elements, such as materiality, the presence of other senses, and an emphasis on the space itself, the site where the book is not just read but also created, shared, and reflected

of the reader’s experience with new technologies only to the degree that these technologies enhance the media’s aesthetic possibilities and the reader’s sensory pleasure. The computer brings a new dimension to the book, which is no longer limited to

the translucence of language, is the way in which literature can be transformed into an art of the book.
Starting from the assumption that the ideal of the book form is an invariable, imperishable artifact,

through the reader’s contribution. In this process the act of reading and interpretation is amplified by the medium’s properties. It is precisely the possibility of continuous transformation that offers its reader a range of experiences that take on a different

its own finite dimensions, but is able to exist in the reader’s mind, endlessly transformed. This is the dream of literature.

In an era when algorithms and big data are concentrating and extending dominance, scholars and authors can

only advance by upscaling. Access to the material base of artworks is a crucial means of survival. In the contemporary book market, the high cost of printing inhibits the speed of publishing and thus the range of cultural expression

. For example, paper books are expensive to transport, and shipping costs continue to rise. Online books are easier to publish, but the form itself becomes static and uncreative, unable to express subtle changes in language.

only proclaim their right to intellectual independence and voice their opposition with words. Literature is a vector for opposition. It is a vector for the expression of the will of the individual and the collective consciousness. Literature produces a counter memory
